unit.title: Meine Familie
unit.sub: Talk about your family — parents, siblings, grandparents, and how to describe them.

steps.title: Father, Mother, Child 👨‍👩‍👧
steps.desc: Family is important in German culture! Learn the core family members.

steps.note: Masculine (der). Also: 'Papa' (casual). 'Vati' is the affectionate form — like 'daddy'. Close to Dutch 'vader' and English 'father'!
Feminine (die). Also: 'Mama' (casual). 'Mutti' is affectionate — like 'mummy'. Notice: 'mein' for der-words, 'meine' for die-words!

steps.example: Mein Bruder ist älter als ich.
steps.exampleEn: My brother is older than me.
steps.note: Masculine (der). Plural: die Brüder (note the umlaut!). Very close to Dutch 'broer' and English 'brother'.
Feminine (die). Plural: die Schwestern. Dutch uses 'zus' — German is closer to English 'sister' here.

steps.title: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Mein vs Meine
steps.text:
Possessive 'my' changes with gender in German:

🔵 der-words → mein: mein Vater, mein Bruder, mein Sohn
🔴 die-words → meine: meine Mutter, meine Schwester, meine Tochter
🟣 das-words → mein: mein Kind, mein Baby, mein Haus

Same pattern for 'your':
dein Vater, deine Mutter, dein Kind

And 'his/her':
sein Vater (his), ihre Mutter (her)

The article gender tells you which form to use!

steps.title: Grandparents & Extended Family 👴👵
steps.desc: German grandparents are often very involved in family life. Learn the extended family tree!

steps.note: Masculine (der). Formal: 'der Großvater'. 'Opa' is the universal casual form — same in Dutch!
Feminine (die). Formal: 'die Großmutter'. 'Oma' is universal — same in Dutch! German grandmothers baking 'Kuchen' (cake) is a beloved stereotype that's often true.

steps.exampleEn: Uncle Hans is coming to visit.
steps.note: Masculine (der). Very similar to Dutch 'oom' but closer to English 'uncle' in form.
Feminine (die). Same word in Dutch! 'Tante' is also used affectionately for older female friends of the family.

steps.title: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 The German family gathering
steps.text:
German families often gather for:

☕ Kaffee und Kuchen (3 PM Sunday tradition)
🎄 Weihnachten (Christmas — THE family holiday)
🐣 Ostern (Easter — big family meals)
🎂 Geburtstag (birthdays — taken very seriously!)

'Die Verwandten' = the relatives
'Die Großeltern' = the grandparents
'Die Geschwister' = siblings (no exact English equivalent!)

Fun fact: 'Geschwister' is one of those wonderful German words that English lacks — it means 'brothers and sisters' in one word.

steps.hint: A collective word for brothers and sisters

steps.title: Big or Small? 📏
steps.desc: Describe your family members — who's older, taller, younger?

steps.note: Means both 'big' and 'tall'. 'Größer' = bigger/taller. The 'ß' is the Eszett — you can also write 'gross'.
Means both 'small' and 'short'. 'Kleiner' = smaller. Same word in Dutch!
Feminine (die). Used for the broader family. 'Das Familienfest' = family celebration. German families tend to be smaller than in many countries — average 1.5 children.
Neuter (das). English loanword! Plural: die Babys. 'Das Kleinkind' = toddler (literally 'small-child').

steps.title: 📏 Describing family
steps.text:
Useful family descriptions:

• Mein Vater ist groß. — My father is tall.
• Meine Mutter ist kleiner als mein Vater. — My mother is shorter than my father.
• Mein Bruder ist älter als ich. — My brother is older than me.
• Meine Schwester ist jünger. — My sister is younger.
• Das Baby ist sehr süß! — The baby is very cute!

Comparatives add '-er':
groß → größer (bigger)
klein → kleiner (smaller)
alt → älter (older — note umlaut!)
jung → jünger (younger — note umlaut!)

steps.q: 'Mein Bruder ist älter als ich' means:
opts: My brother is taller than me
My brother is older than me
My brother is smaller than me
My brother is younger than me
steps.ans: My brother is older than me

steps.hint: My sister is younger than me

steps.title: Family Reunion 💫
steps.desc: Put it all together — describe your whole family in German!

steps.title: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Describe your family
steps.text:
A full family description pattern:

Das ist meine Familie. — This is my family.
Mein Vater heißt Klaus und meine Mutter heißt Petra. — My father's name is Klaus and my mother's name is Petra.
Ich habe einen Bruder — er heißt Max. — I have a brother — his name is Max.
Meine Oma und mein Opa wohnen in Hamburg. — My grandma and grandpa live in Hamburg.
Wir sind zu fünft. — There are five of us.
Ich liebe meine Familie! — I love my family!

'Ich liebe...' = I love... (for people and things)

steps.q: Someone says 'Hast du Geschwister?' — what are they asking?
How do you say 'I love my family' in German?
